meta.current-phase: Hardening complete: P1-P6 all done. Security, backend stability, frontend quality, infrastructure, tests/CI, and cleanup phases delivered. System ready for production hardening review.

status-summary.done: Backend, user frontend, admin frontend, bot and deploy surface exist as an MVP. VPN topology has moved toward VPNNode/VPNRoute with domain-aware routing policy. All 8 implementation phases (Phase-1 through Phase-8) are done. All 6 hardening phases (P1-P6) are done: security, backend stability, frontend quality, infrastructure, tests/CI, cleanup. API versioned at /api/v1. Alembic migrations initialized. CI/CD pipeline configured. 51 security and quality fixes applied across 6 commits.
status-summary.blocked-or-weak: Handshake anomaly detection remains in detect-first mode; automatic blocking thresholds not yet enabled. Alembic migration workflow coexists with SQLModel.create_all during transition period. Token blacklist requires Redis — graceful degradation in place but not tested under Redis failure.

WS-001.name: AI Handoff and Documentation Accuracy
WS-001.goal: Keep GRACE artifacts aligned with the implemented system and planned migration path.

WS-002.name: Security and Deployment Hardening
WS-002.goal: Reduce operational risk in secrets handling, admin access and deployment defaults.

WS-003.name: VPN Topology and Provisioning Stability
WS-003.goal: Finish stabilizing VPNNode/VPNRoute-based provisioning and analytics invariants.

WS-004.name: Flexible Routing Policy Migration
WS-004.goal: Introduce a routing decision engine that supports exact-domain, wildcard-domain, DNS-bound IP and CIDR-based classification without breaking the current RU-vs-non-RU baseline.

WS-005.name: Verification and Testability
WS-005.goal: Raise routing, VPN and admin verification to a level safe for autonomous execution.

WS-006.name: Manual Internal Client Provisioning
WS-006.goal: Replace raw host-only client config generation with a backend-driven internal-access flow that produces the same working configs as the web path.

WS-007.name: Device-Bound Access Control and Anti-Sharing
WS-007.goal: Bind subscriptions to tracked devices with per-plan limits, peer-level revoke or rotate operations, and explicit detection or enforcement for suspicious handshake reuse.

M-001.name: backend-core
M-001.purpose: Configuration, security, DB lifecycle and bootstrap primitives.

M-002.name: users
M-002.purpose: User auth, profile and Telegram-linked identity flows.

M-003.name: vpn
M-003.purpose: VPN provisioning, config generation and topology-aware client assignment.

M-004.name: billing
M-004.purpose: Plans, subscriptions, payments and webhook processing.

M-005.name: referrals
M-005.purpose: Referral codes, links and bonus accounting.

M-006.name: admin-api
M-006.purpose: Privileged analytics and operational control endpoints.

M-007.name: routing
M-007.purpose: Host-level split-tunneling, ipset/iptables coordination and routing health inspection.

M-008.name: tasks
M-008.purpose: Background scheduling and recurring maintenance jobs.

M-009.name: frontend-user
M-009.purpose: Main user UI for auth, config delivery, subscription and referrals.

M-010.name: frontend-admin
M-010.purpose: Admin UI for analytics, plans, users, servers and routing surfaces.

M-011.name: telegram-bot
M-011.purpose: Telegram bot orchestration over backend HTTP APIs.

M-012.name: deploy-surface
M-012.purpose: Install, deploy, nginx and compose surfaces that shape production behavior.

M-013.name: route-policy-resolver
M-013.purpose: Decide routing target using explicit domain rules, DNS-bound addresses, explicit CIDR rules and fallback policy.

M-014.name: domain-rule-store
M-014.purpose: Persist exact, wildcard and priority-ordered routing rules for domains and CIDRs.

M-015.name: dns-observer
M-015.purpose: Track domain-to-IP bindings from DNS resolution and maintain TTL-aware policy cache updates.

M-016.name: route-decision-api
M-016.purpose: Expose admin controls and inspection endpoints for routing policy rules and effective decisions.

M-017.name: route-sync-runtime
M-017.purpose: Translate resolved policy decisions into host-level routing sets and incremental refresh actions.

M-018.name: manual-access-policy
M-018.purpose: Grant and reuse internal non-billable access for operator-managed users without bypassing the main subscription and provisioning model.

M-019.name: manual-client-cli
M-019.purpose: Create or reuse an internal user, ensure complimentary access, provision a VPN client, and export a working config through backend CLI.

M-020.name: device-registry
M-020.purpose: Persist user devices, device lifecycle state, peer linkage and security-relevant device events.

M-021.name: device-access-policy
M-021.purpose: Enforce per-plan device limits, decide slot reuse rules, and coordinate revoke, rotate and block actions for device-bound access.

M-022.name: device-provisioning-api
M-022.purpose: Expose user-facing API endpoints for device creation, listing, config retrieval, rotation and revocation.

M-023.name: handshake-anomaly-detector
M-023.purpose: Observe peer handshake or endpoint changes, score suspicious reuse patterns and trigger explicit anti-sharing events.

M-024.name: device-admin-control
M-024.purpose: Expose admin controls for viewing devices, fraud signals, blocking peers and revoking compromised device access.

M-025.name: device-audit-log
M-025.purpose: Keep an explicit audit trail of device creation, rotation, revocation, blocking and handshake anomaly escalation.

DF-001.name: Domain-first routing decision
DF-001.summary: Admin-defined domain policy is evaluated first. Exact and wildcard domain rules override generic RU IP heuristics.

DF-002.name: DNS binding to runtime route set
DF-002.summary: DNS-derived IPs for a matched domain are cached and synchronized into the runtime route set with TTL awareness.

DF-003.name: Fallback CIDR classification
DF-003.summary: If no domain policy applies, explicit CIDR rules and the RU baseline determine whether traffic stays local or exits via DE.

DF-004.name: Provisioning and config consistency
DF-004.summary: VPN provisioning and routing runtime share the same decision policy so client expectations match traffic behavior.

DF-005.name: Admin observability of policy decisions
DF-005.summary: Operators can inspect why a domain or IP was routed to RU, DE, DIRECT or DEFAULT.

DF-006.name: Manual internal client provisioning
DF-006.summary: Operator-run client creation resolves or creates an internal user, grants non-billable access, provisions a VPN client and returns the same config shape as the web flow.

DF-007.name: Config parity between web and CLI paths
DF-007.summary: Web registration and operator CLI issuance must converge on the same VPNService-based topology selection and config rendering path.

DF-008.name: Manual client idempotency and reprovision safety
DF-008.summary: Re-running manual client issuance for the same identity should reuse or explicitly reprovision state instead of creating silent duplicate active clients.

DF-009.name: Device creation under plan limits
DF-009.summary: A logged-in user requests a new device; device-access policy loads the active subscription and plan, checks active device count against the limit and provisions a distinct peer or rejects the request.

DF-010.name: Device-specific config lifecycle
DF-010.summary: Config download, rotation and revocation operate on a concrete device record so each official device has its own peer, address and audit trail.

DF-011.name: Handshake anomaly detection and scoring
DF-011.summary: Runtime handshake or endpoint observations are converted into structured device security events and a suspicion score that can later justify soft or hard enforcement.

DF-012.name: Admin peer enforcement
DF-012.summary: An admin reviews device and anomaly state, then blocks, revokes or unblocks a specific device peer without touching unrelated devices on the same user account.

DF-013.name: Legacy client migration to primary device
DF-013.summary: Existing single-client users are migrated to a deterministic primary device record so the new device-bound model does not orphan current subscriptions or peers.

Phase-1.name: Policy model and safe compatibility layer
Phase-1.step-1: Create M-014 rule entities and schemas for exact-domain, wildcard-domain and CIDR rules.
Phase-1.step-2: Create M-013 resolver with deterministic precedence: domain exact, domain wildcard, DNS-bound IP, explicit CIDR, RU baseline, default.
Phase-1.step-3: Keep current RU-vs-non-RU behavior as fallback so rollout remains backward compatible.

Phase-2.name: Runtime synchronization and DNS binding
Phase-2.step-1: Extend M-017 runtime sync so policy changes become incremental host-level updates instead of full resets where possible.
Phase-2.step-2: Add M-015 DNS observer and bounded cache with TTL-driven expiry and refresh.
Phase-2.step-3: Introduce decision logging anchors for every policy branch.

Phase-3.name: Admin control plane and operator visibility
Phase-3.step-1: Extend M-016 with CRUD for domain rules and read-only decision inspection endpoints.
Phase-3.step-2: Expose policy and resolution state in the admin frontend without changing the user-facing product flow yet.
Phase-3.step-3: Add migration-safe analytics so legacy VPNServer-based views do not silently misrepresent routing state.

Phase-4.name: Hardening and rollout gates
Phase-4.step-1: Run routing-focused verification on exact-domain, wildcard-domain, TTL expiry and fallback branches.
Phase-4.step-2: Gate production rollout on host-level safety review for ipset or nftables updates.
Phase-4.step-3: Only after evidence is stable, make domain-aware policy the preferred routing control surface.

Phase-5.name: Manual internal access and CLI provisioning parity
Phase-5.step-1: Add an internal non-billable access model that remains visible in normal user, subscription and VPN state instead of bypassing billing and provisioning.
Phase-5.step-2: Add backend CLI orchestration that creates or resolves the internal user, ensures access, provisions the client and exports config through VPNService.
Phase-5.step-3: Convert deploy/create-client.sh into a thin wrapper over the backend CLI so manual configs no longer use raw host-only generation.
Phase-5.step-4: Verify parity between manually issued configs and web-issued configs, including idempotency and restart-safe peer persistence.

Phase-6.name: Device registry and limit-aware migration
Phase-6.step-1: Add per-plan device limit fields and a first-class device registry with lifecycle state, last seen metadata and audit-friendly identifiers.
Phase-6.step-2: Refactor VPNClient linkage from a unique per-user record to a device-bound peer while preserving user linkage for compatibility queries.
Phase-6.step-3: Migrate existing users to a deterministic primary device so no active peer is lost during the model transition.

Phase-7.name: User device management and device-bound provisioning
Phase-7.step-1: Expose user-facing device APIs for listing devices, creating a new device, revoking a device and rotating a leaked or compromised config.
Phase-7.step-2: Update dashboard and config delivery flows so each device receives its own peer and config instead of reusing a single account-wide peer.
Phase-7.step-3: Ensure plan limits are enforced before provisioning and that revoke frees a slot while rotate preserves the same logical device identity.

Phase-8.name: Anti-sharing detection and admin enforcement
Phase-8.step-1: Add structured handshake or endpoint observation so suspicious concurrent reuse of one device peer becomes visible as stable security events.
Phase-8.step-2: Implement suspicion scoring and staged enforcement: detect first, then allow admin review, then optionally enable automatic block thresholds once false-positive risk is understood.
Phase-8.step-3: Expose admin controls for block, unblock, revoke and rotate actions on specific device peers with audit logging.

Phase-9.name: Critical Security Fixes (P1)
Phase-9.step-1: Block default secret_key with ValueError instead of warning.
Phase-9.step-2: Require explicit DATA_ENCRYPTION_KEY, remove JWT-secret fallback for Fernet.
Phase-9.step-3: Fix auth bypass in admin_users_router (Depends() instead of = None).
Phase-9.step-4: Remove sshpass from all deploy scripts, require SSH key-based auth.
Phase-9.step-5: Remove base64 encoding of secrets in install.sh.
Phase-9.step-6: Remove plaintext admin credentials file creation.
Phase-9.step-7: Remove hardcoded production IPs, use env vars.
Phase-9.step-8: Remove port 8000 external mapping from docker-compose.

Phase-10.name: Backend Stability Fixes (P2)
Phase-10.step-1: Replace datetime.utcnow() with datetime.now(timezone.utc) in 40+ locations.
Phase-10.step-2: Initialize Alembic with async support and generate baseline migration.
Phase-10.step-4: Fix _reprovision_client to remove old WG peer after adding new one.
Phase-10.step-5: Fix server.current_clients counter decrement on subscription expiry.
Phase-10.step-6: Fix _get_or_provision_user_client to return proper HTTP 400/409 errors.
Phase-10.step-7: Document circular dependency chains in app/core/__init__.py.
Phase-10.step-8: Split database.py (1068 lines) into database.py + migrations.py.

Phase-11.name: Frontend Security & Quality (P3)
Phase-11.step-1: Add httpOnly secure cookies to auth endpoints alongside Bearer tokens.
Phase-11.step-2: Fix refresh token interceptor to use configured axios instance.
Phase-11.step-3: Eliminate 53 any usages in admin frontend with 18 typed interfaces.
Phase-11.step-5: Fix PWA cache regex to match relative /api/ paths.
Phase-11.step-7: Fix i18next escapeValue to prevent XSS via translations.
Phase-11.step-8: Add refresh token interceptor to admin frontend.

Phase-12.name: Infrastructure Hardening (P4)
Phase-12.step-1: Fix deploy idempotency — preserve WG keys on re-deploy.
Phase-12.step-3: Add healthchecks for nginx, frontend, frontend-admin.
Phase-12.step-4: Add resource limits to all docker-compose containers.
Phase-12.step-5: Replace hardcoded eth0 with dynamic interface detection in iptables.
Phase-12.step-6: Add security headers to nginx (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options).
Phase-12.step-7: Add rate limiting to nginx (5r/m login, 30r/m API).
Phase-12.step-8: Add deploy/backup-db.sh for automated pg_dump with 7-backup retention.
Phase-12.step-9: Restrict UFW FORWARD to deny + explicit AWG interface rules.
Phase-12.step-10: Verify no eval injection in install.sh.

Phase-13.name: Tests & CI/CD (P5)
Phase-13.step-1: Add test_security.py (10 tests for JWT, password, Fernet, blacklist).
Phase-13.step-2: Add auth rejection tests (401/403 for unauthenticated).
Phase-13.step-3: Add test_billing_service.py (5 tests for subscription lifecycle).
Phase-13.step-4: Add test_scheduler.py (4 tests for expiry, cleanup, anomaly detection).
Phase-13.step-5: Add test_referrals.py (7 tests for code, linking, bonus, stats).
Phase-13.step-8: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml with lint, test, build jobs.

Phase-14.name: Cleanup & Polish (P6)
Phase-14.step-1: Split vpn/service.py (1112 lines) into service.py, provisioning.py, config.py, topology.py.
Phase-14.step-5: Remove dead App.tsx in admin frontend.
Phase-14.step-6: Migrate all API routes to /api/v1 prefix.
Phase-14.step-7: Move hardcoded krotvpn.com URL to Settings.frontend_url.

observability-notes.item: Every route decision must include a stable reason code: domain_exact, domain_wildcard, dns_bound_ip, cidr_rule, ru_baseline, default.
DNS binding refresh must emit stable logs for add, refresh, expire and conflict branches.
Runtime sync must expose whether a rule was applied incrementally, skipped, or required a full refresh.
Manual client issuance must emit stable markers for request, identity resolution, complimentary access grant, provisioning outcome and config export.
Device lifecycle events must emit stable markers for create, limit rejection, revoke, rotate, block and unblock actions.
Handshake anomaly detection must log endpoint changes and suspicion escalation with structured fields rather than prose-only operator notes.

scenario.name: Known Russian domain
scenario.summary: Client requests a domain matched by an exact or wildcard RU rule. Resolver returns RU without depending on the current resolved IP.

scenario.name: Known foreign domain behind changing CDN IPs
scenario.summary: Domain rule sends traffic to DE. DNS observer keeps the changing IP set aligned so CDN drift does not collapse policy correctness.

scenario.name: Unknown domain with no explicit rule
scenario.summary: Resolver falls back to explicit CIDR policy and finally the existing RU baseline, preserving current behavior until policy coverage grows.

scenario.name: Operator creates internal client for family use
scenario.summary: Admin runs the manual client command, backend resolves or creates an internal identity, grants non-billable access, provisions a VPN client and returns a config that matches the normal user flow.

scenario.name: Repeated manual issue for the same client name
scenario.summary: CLI resolves the same internal identity and reuses or explicitly reprovisions the existing client rather than creating hidden duplicate state with a different peer or IP.

scenario.name: User with one-device plan adds a second device
scenario.summary: The first device stays active, the second creation request is rejected before peer provisioning, and the response tells the user to revoke an existing device or upgrade the plan.

scenario.name: User rotates a leaked device config
scenario.summary: The same logical device remains in the registry, but its old peer is revoked, a new peer is provisioned and the leaked config stops working without affecting the user’s other devices.

scenario.name: One config is copied to multiple people
scenario.summary: Runtime observations show repeated endpoint churn or near-concurrent peer reuse, anti-sharing signals are recorded, and the device can be escalated from warning to block without disabling the whole account.

risks.item: Domain-aware routing is incomplete if DNS bindings are not captured before traffic reaches only-IP enforcement points.
CDN and shared-IP domains can cause false sharing unless rule priority and conflict handling are explicit.
Host-level rule churn can degrade stability if runtime sync is not incremental and bounded.
Admin analytics can drift from real routing behavior if legacy VPNServer assumptions remain unadjusted.
Manual client issuance will remain unreliable if deploy/create-client.sh keeps generating peers and configs outside backend topology and subscription state.
Internal non-billable access can pollute billing analytics unless complimentary access is explicitly marked and reported.
Unsafe reprovision rules could create duplicate active clients or mismatched peer state for the same internal identity.
Device migration can orphan active peers if the current one-client-per-user invariant is removed without a deterministic primary-device bootstrap.
False positives in handshake anomaly detection can block honest mobile users if automatic enforcement is enabled before the detector is tuned.
Blocked devices must not silently free a plan slot too early, or abuse loops will become cheaper instead of harder.
